coredata.dc:title: Improved the specificity of peroxidase-like carbonized polydopamine nanotubes with high nitrogen doping for glutathione detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tian
Li, Haidong
Liu, Miaoxia
Zhou, Han
Zhang, Zhicheng
Yu, Cao
Wang, Chengyin
Wang, Guoxiu
coredata.dc:description: The introduction of nitrogen heteroatoms into enzyme-like carbon materials is an effective strategy to enhance both catalytic activity and specificity. Herein, we described the preparation of the carbonized polydopamine nanotubes (C-DPANTs) using sacrificial halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) templates. The resulting nitrogen doped C-PDANTs (N-doped C-PDANTs) exhibit solely the peroxidase-like activity owing to the high N doping. N-doped C-PDANTs could catalyze the reduction of H2O2 to produce OH● radicals, which contributed to the oxidation of 3,3’, 5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrates. Moreover, the catalytic mechanism of N-doped C-PDANTs was further revealed by electrochemical measurements. N-doped C-PDANTs with pyridinic N and graphitic N compositions possess the capability to accelerate the electron transfer, thus facilitating the reduction of H2O2. The application of N-doped C-PDANTs were demonstrated in the sensitive detection of glutathione (GSH) in serum samples. Therefore, the present study provides a facile way to prepared peroxidase-like nanomaterials with high specificity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon nanomaterials
Peroxidase-like activity
Polydopamine
Colorimetric detection
Glutathione

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of GSH by colorimetric method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the preparation of N-doped C-PDANTs. SEM images of (B) HNTs, (C) HNTs@PDA and (D) N-doped C-PDANTs.
(A) FTIR spectra of different nanotubes in the preparation process. (B) XPS spectrum of N-doped C-PDANTs. C 1s (C) and N 1s (D) XPS spectra of N-doped C-PDANTs.

(A) UV–vis spectra of different reaction systems: (a) TMB, (b) TMB-H2O2, (c) TMB-H2O2-PDANTs, (d) TMB-N-doped C-PDANTs-H2O2 and (e) TMB-N-doped C-PDANTs-H2O2-GSH in 0.2 M acetate buffer solution (pH 4.0). The insets are digital photographs for the corresponding reaction systems. (B) ESR spectra of hydroxyl radicals in different reaction systems. (C) The absorbance-time curves for TMB oxidation catalyzed by different amounts of N-doped C-PDANTs (An initial period of 60 s was selected because the R2 coefficients were close to 1 during this initial rete period after a linear-regression analysis). (D) The specific activity of N-doped C-PDANTs derived from (C).

(A) CVs of (a, b) bare GCE, (c, d) Nafion-GCE, (e, f) N-doped C-PDANTs/Nafion-GCE without and with the presence of 1 mM H2O2 in 50 mM PBS solution (pH7.0) (Scan rate: 40 mV/s). (B) Chronoamperograms of CNTs/Nafion-GCE obtained in 50 mM PBS (pH7.4) containing different concentration of H2O2. (C) Linear curve of i(H2O2) / i (PBS) vs. t1/2 derived from the chronoamperometric data of 2 mM H2O2 and PBS. (D) Chronoamperometric i-t curve of N-doped C-PDANTs/Nafion-GCE with adding H2O2 into 50 mM PBS solution (pH7.0). Inset is the calibration curve of current responses and H2O2 concentrations.
(A) Schematic diagram of GSH detection based on the peroxidase-like activity of N-doped C-PDANTs. (B) Plots of ΔA vs. GSH concentration. (C) Linear plots of ΔA vs. GSH concentration.
GSH detection in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Improved the specificity of peroxidase-like carbonized polydopamine nanotubes with high nitrogen doping for glutathione detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225127, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225127, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, University of Technology Sydney, Sydney, New South Wales 2007, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Science, University of Technology Sydney, Sydney, New South Wales 2007, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The introduction of nitrogen heteroatoms into enzyme-like carbon materials is an effective strategy to enhance both catalytic activity and specificity. Herein, we described the preparation of the carbonized polydopamine nanotubes (C-DPANTs) using sacrificial halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) templates. The resulting nitrogen doped C-PDANTs (N-doped C-PDANTs) exhibit solely the peroxidase-like activity owing to the high N doping. N-doped C-PDANTs could catalyze the reduction of H2O2 to produce OH● radicals, which contributed to the oxidation of 3,3’, 5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrates. Moreover, the catalytic mechanism of N-doped C-PDANTs was further revealed by electrochemical measurements. N-doped C-PDANTs with pyridinic N and graphitic N compositions possess the capability to accelerate the electron transfer, thus facilitating the reduction of H2O2. The application of N-doped C-PDANTs were demonstrated in the sensitive detection of glutathione (GSH) in serum samples. Therefore, the present study provides a facile way to prepared peroxidase-like nanomaterials with high specificity.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanohorns (CNHs), graphene and fullerence are typical synthetic carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) with unique structure, high mechanical strength, excellent electrical conductivity, high chemical stability and biocompatibility [1,2]. Such intrinsic properties allow us to apply these materials in different research fields [3], such as electrochemical/biological sensor, environmental monitoring, artificial biomaterial, electrocatalysis, energy conversion and storage and so on [4–8]. Furthermore, the functionalization of CNMs by noncovalent or covalent chemical modifications can not only enhance their intrinsic properties, but also introduce new ones [9]. In this context, doping single or multiple heteroatoms in the sp2 lattice of graphite carbon is recognized as an effective strategy for the functionalization of CNMs. The doping of foreign atoms can be achieved by either the physical absorption or the chemical bonding in various configurations in CNMs [10]. It thus provides a versatile approach to modify CNMs.

The most common-used heteroatoms for CNMs doping are O and N due to their abundance and strong atomic bonding with carbon. Additionally, it has been proved that electron-rich O and N atoms doped in carbon matrix can change electronic arrangement, increase electron donating property and create active sites with high catalytic activity [11]. For example, N-doped CNMs were reported to have higher electro-catalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) or oxygen evolution reaction (OER), showing better performance in the design of fuel cells and metal–air batteries [12,13]. In fact, the catalytic activity is highly depending on the ratio and form of the N dopants in CNMs. Specifically, pyridinic N and graphitic N are the two major compositions in N-doped CNMs for initiating the electro-catalysis of oxygen reduction [14]. Therefore, these findings have offered the guidelines for the rational design of N-doped CNMs for electro-catalysis [15].

In addition to the excellent electro-catalytic activity of N-doped CNMs, their peroxidase-like activity has been studied in several works [16–19]. Meanwhile, N-doped CNMs might exhibit other enzyme-like activities, including oxidase-like, catalase-like and superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity [20]. Although the multiple enzyme-like activities can extend the applications in cascade catalysis, the specificity, as an intrinsic character of enzyme, is not fully addressed by using such N-doped CNMs [16]. To improve the specificity of enzyme-like materials, the combination with molecular imprinted polymers, or the high N doping was reported [18,21]. However, the formation of polymer layers on the surface of the enzyme-like materials may inevitably cover the active sites and hinder the molecular transmission process on the materials [22]. Since both N doping and graphitic structure play crucial roles to prepare the enzyme-like materials, carefully selected precursors are still required. N-doped CNMs without the successful carbonization and the proper N doping do not have any enzyme-like activities [20].

PDA is a well-known adhesive pigment that mimics the adhesive foot protein of marine mussels [23]. The catechol groups and amine groups on PDA show strong chelating ability to transition metal ions, providing reactive sites for hybrid material preparation [24]. Also, the catalytic activity of pristine PDA materials was found in a few reports [25–27]. In present work, we have prepared highly N-doped C-PDANTs as the peroxidase-like nanomaterials. PDA as the only precursor was firstly formed on HNTs templates via the self-polymerization of dopamine. Then, the carbonization of PDA could lead to the easy pyrolysis with the conversion to sp2 graphitic carbon and the production of high N doping and porosity (Fig. 1 A). The resulting N-doped C-PDANTs show high specificity to peroxidase-like activity, rather than other enzyme-like activities. The reduction of H2O2 at less negative potential was also reported for N-doped C-PDACNTs, confirming the effect of pyridinic N and graphitic N on the catalytic activity. Finally, we have developed a colorimetric method for GSH detection based on N-doped C-PDANTs.

N-doped C-PDANTs were prepared through PDA coating on HNTs [28], followed by HNTs template etching and the carbonization. Typically, 20 mg HNTs were firstly dispersed in 70 mL 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.5) with the ultra-sonication for 2 min. Then, the formation of polymer films on the HNTs surface were achieved by adding 20 mg DA monomers into the HNTs dispersion under the mild stirring. The polymerization was allowed to proceed for 24 h at room temperature. After that, the HNTs@PDA hybrid nanotubes were collected from the resulted solution by the centrifugation and washed with deionized water for 3 times. The as-prepared HNTs@PDA hybrid nanotubes and 2 mL HCl (10 %, v/v) solution was firstly mixed in a plastic tube, and then the mixture was continuously stirred as 1 mL HF (50 %, v/v) solution wad added drop by drop using a pipette. The solution was kept stirring at room temperature for 24 h to remove the HNT templates. PDANTs were obtained through the centrifugation and washing in deionized water for 6 cycles, and the freeze-dried products were prepared for the subsequent carbonization. Finally, N-doped C-PDANTs were produced by annealing at 800 °C for 1 h in highly-pure Ar gas with a heating rate 1 °C/min.

The investigations for the peroxidase-like activity of N-doped C-PDANTs was based on mode TMB/H2O2 reaction system. Specifically, 150 μL 4 mM TMB, 1500 μL 100 mM H2O2 and 100 μL N-doped C-PDANTs suspension (1.0 mg/mL) were added into a vial and diluted to 4 mL with 0.2 M acetate buffer solution (pH 4.0). The solution was mixed on a vortex mixer and incubated in a room-temperature bath for 3 min. UV–vis spectrum of the mixed solution was then measured in a quartz cuvette. The kinetic measurements were conducted by recording absorbance of the solution at 652 nm in time course mode on the spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH by colorimetric method
ce:section.ce:para: For GSH detection, the same reaction system was employed in 0.2 M acetate buffer solution (pH 4.0), which consists of 150 μL TMB (4 mM), 1500 μL H2O2 (100 mM) and 100 μL N-doped C-PDANTs suspension (1.0 mg/mL). After the chromogenic reaction, varied concentrations of GSH were added into 4 mL of test solution. The absorbance changes of UV–vis spectra at 652 nm were used to monitor the evolution by adding GSH at different concentrations.

The facile self-polymerization of dopamine to form PDA in a weak alkaline solution with the presence of dissolved oxygen has been widely used for mussel-inspired surface coating on nearly all kinds of substrates [29]. However, the mechanism details behind the polymerization of dopamine are unclear and remain an attractive area in scientific community [23]. Generally, the polymerization is initiated by the oxidation of dopamine to produce dopamine-quinone, followed by the cyclization and isomerization reactions, culminated with the formation of PDA [30]. As depicted in Fig. 1A, such polymerization will lead to a conformal coating on HNTs surface by adding dopamine to the suspension of HNTs. Then N-doped C-PDANTs were prepared by the template etching and carbonization processes. Fig. 1B, C and D show the corresponding morphologies characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). Both images of HNTs and HNTs@PDA nanotubes display obvious tubular structure. But HNTs@PDA nanotubes show rough surface and have an average diameter of 90 ± 10 nm (Fig. 1C), while the average diameter of the pristine HNTs is 70 ± 10 nm (Fig. 1B). A hollow lumen was clearly visible in each N-doped C-PDANTs (Fig. 1D), revealing the successful etching of HNTs templates. But single wall was left after the etching and carbonization treatments with thickness of ∼20 nm. It may be due to the blocking of the dopamine diffusion into the inner lumen of HNTs, or the damage of PDA formed on the inner face. Besides, N-doped C-PDANTs show less cylindrical and stiff shape because of the distortion of polymer layer in the preparation, but larger lumen (∼70 nm) than that of HNTs.

The formation of PDA on the surface of HNTs was also verified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The characteristic absorption bands of HNTs could be found in Fig. 2 A, which the absorption peaks at 3694 cm−1 and 3620 cm−1 were related to the OH stretching of hydroxyl groups, the ones at 1024 cm-1 and 907 cm−1 were assigned to the Si–O stretching and the OH deformation of the aluminum hydroxyl groups, and the ones at 534 cm−1 and 470 cm−1 were originated from the stretching vibration of Al–O–Si and Si–O–Si, respectively [31–33]. Even after coating PDA on HNTs, on one hand, these characteristic peaks remained detectable. On the other hand, new absorption bands for DPA appeared in FTIR that the peaks at 3376 cm−1 and 3229 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of NH and OH, the ones at 1611 cm−1 and 1508 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration of aromatic ring and –NH, the one locates at 1284 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of phenolic CO [34–37]. Finally, only the absorption peaks of PDA were observed after etching the template. All these results indicate the success of PDA coating and HNTs removal.

However, the absorption bands became too weak to be identified in FTIR of N-doped C-PDANTs. A broad diffraction peak at 24.4° was observed in XPS pattern (Fig. S1A), in consistent with the (002) plane of graphite in N-doped carbonized materials [18,38]. This result suggests that the annealing of PDA NTs in an inert Ar gas has led to the pyrolysis and carbonization of the polymer. The compositions of N-doped C-PDANTs were further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In Fig. 2B, the signals of C 1, N 1 and O1 were found from XPS survey scan, while there was no obvious signals of Al or Si element. This reveals the complete removal of HNTs in the etching step. It should be noted that atomic percentage for N in N-doped C-PDANTs was detected to be 11.79 %, which is higher than that of the other N-doped carbon nanomaterials reported in other works (Table S1). This might be related to the severe dehydration in the carbonization, resulting in the reducing of O content and the raising of N content. The high-resolution XPS spectra were further analyzed to verify the configurations of C and N in N-doped C-PDANTs (Fig. 2D and C). The carbonization of PDA at different temperatures has been nicely demonstrated in the work of Ai et al., in which the facile conversion of sp3 C to sp2 C and the tuning of N composition were observed [39]. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra reveal three C 1s peaks for N-doped C-PDANTs annealing at 800 °C as shown in Fig. 2C, representing sp2 at 284.6 eV, CN at 285.5 eV and CO at 288.9 eV, respectively [40]. The high-resolution N 1s spectra were fitted with two distinct peaks. The peak at 398.4 eV could be attributed to pyridinic N, while the other one at 400.9 eV was ascribed to graphitic N [41,42]. The porosity of N-doped C-PDANTs was investigated by N2 sorption isotherms (Fig. S1B). The isotherm showed the characteristics of type-IV curve with H4 hysteresis loop at high pressure region, revealing the presence of micro/meso-pores on N-doped C-PDANTs. The average pore diameter and BET surface area were measured to be 4.5 nm and 392.1 m2/g, respectively. Taken together, with the formation of sp2 C, high-level of N doping and porous structure, one can expect that N-doped C-PDANTs possess the improved catalytic activity for our next investigations.

N-doped C-PDANTs were then applied to evaluate their peroxidase-like activity. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the addition of N-doped C-PDANTs into TMB and H2O2 system did result in significant change of the solution color from colorless to blue due to the oxidation of TMB substrate. This was also confirmed in the corresponding UV–vis spectra, which the maximum absorption peak was observed at 652 nm (curve d). In contrast, H2O2 (curve b) and PDANTs (curve c) could not induce noticeable absorbance under the same conditions. Other enzyme-like activities were also examined in Fig. S2. N-doped C-PDANTs show neither oxidase-like and catalase-like activity, nor SOD-like activity in the relevant reaction system. Besides, N-doped C-PDANTs were able to catalyze the oxidation of other peroxidase substrates, like 2,2'-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid ammonium salt) (ABTS) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD), by H2O2 in the acetate buffer, producing a greenish blue (maximum absorbance at 415 nm) and yellow (maximum absorbance at 450 nm) color, respectively (Fig. S3). These results reveal the exclusive peroxidase-like activity of N-doped C-PDANTs to H2O2. Interestingly, the blue color of TMB-N-doped C-PDANTs-H2O2 system faded rapidly by adding GSH and its absorbance at 652 nm decreased greatly as well (curve e). Such phenomenon can be explained by the inhibition of the catalytic oxidation of TMB by of GSH [43]. The reaction conditions for N-doped C-PDANTs in the system were optimized by varying pH and temperature. N-doped C-PDANTs show the highest peroxidase-like activity in the acetate buffer solution with pH = 4.0 at room temperature (Fig. S4). Unlike the natural peroxidase, for example horse radish peroxidase (HPR), N-doped C-PDANTs could still keep similar catalytic activity even after the preincubation in some harsh environments, such as strong acid/base, high temperature and high salinity (Fig.S5), exhibiting much better performance. The catalytic behavior of N-doped C-PDANTs was further investigated through electron spin resonance (ESR). Strong DMPO/OH● adduct signals were detected in the DMPO-H2O2-N-doped C-PDANTs system, rather than in the control groups (Fig. 3B). It thus means that the generation of OH● radicals from the decomposition of H2O2 catalyzed by N-doped C-PDANTs, similar to the way in DMPO-H2O2-HPR system [44]. The generated OH● radicals can oxidise TMB to form blue oxTMB.

To calculate the catalytic kinetic constants of N-doped C-PDANTs, the steady-state kinetic assays were performed by varying the concentration of either TMB or H2O2 substrate in a certain range (see in the Supporting Information, Fig. S6). The resulting kinetic constants were compared with those of HPR and other N-doped nanomaterials as listed in Table S2. The K m value of N-doped C-PDANTs toward TMB is much lower than that of HPR, but the one toward H2O2 is higher than the value of HPR. Since the K m value could be used to determine the enzyme affinity to substrate, these data reveal that N-doped C-PDANTs show a stronger affinity to TMB than HPR, and a lower affinity to H2O2. That is why a higher concentration of H2O2 is needed to achieve the maximal activity of V max. Moreover, N-doped C-PDANTs exhibit higher V max values for both TMB and H2O2 than HPR. The kinetic data obtained by N-doped C-PDANTs are also promising in contrast with the ones of other N-doped carbon nanomaterials. To better demonstrate the catalytic activity, the specific activity (SA) defined as activity units per gram of nanomaterials was measured by a standardized method (see in the Supporting Information). The SA value is calculated to be 549 U/g (Fig. 3C and D), which is comparable to the ones of other nanomaterials with peroxidase-like activity (Table S3). We also studied the influences of the carbonization temperature and time on SA, respectively (Fig. S7). The highest SA value was obtained from N-doped C-PDANTs annealed at 800 °C for 1 h, due to the successful sp2 C conversion, optimal N composition and micro/meso-pore formation in this condition [39].

It has been found that the N-doping could selectively enhance the peroxidase-like activity in carbon materials [16,18]. Such intrinsic peroxidase-like activity can be attributed to the composition of pyridinic N and graphitic N in C-PDANTs annealed at 800 °C, which provide active sites for H2O2 reduction. For verifying this speculation, we have carried out the electrochemistry measurements to study the H2O2 reduction on N-doped C-PDANTs/Nafion-GCE. Fig. 4 A displays the comparison of CVs of different electrodes (i.e. bare GCE, Nafion-GCE and N-doped C-PDANTs/Nafion GCE) without and with the presence of 1 mM H2O2 in 50 mM PBS solution (pH7.4). Remarkable reduction peak was observed on N-doped C-PDANTs/Nafion GCE in the presence of H2O2, while no distinct reduction signals were detected on the other two electrodes. Moreover, the reduction of H2O2 occurs at -0.26 V vs Ag/AgCl on N-doped C-PDANTs/Nafion GCE, which is less negative than that of Au NPs/N-GQD or BSA-RuO2 NPs modified electrode in previous reports [45,46]. Indeed, the improvement of onset potential by pyridinic N and the increasing of limiting current density by graphitic N could facilitate the electron transfer from GCE to H2O2 [12]. The effect of scan rate on the reduction wave was investigated in the range from 0.02 to 0.2 V/s and the reduction peak current increased linearly with the square root of the scan rate. This reduction behavior evidences a diffusion-controlled process on the modified GCE. The analysis of variation of reduction peak potential with scan rate suggests a one-electron transfer process for H2O2 reduction on the electrode (see in the Supporting Information, Fig. S8). The corresponding equation for reductively decomposition of H2O2 is given bellow [47]:
(1)
H 2
O 2
+
e
-
→
O H
·
+
O H
-

Then, the electro-catalytic rate constant (K cat) was evaluated by chronoamperometric measurements at a fixed potential of -0.30 V vs Ag/AgCl in PBS buffer solution containing different concentrations of H2O2 (Fig. 4B). The K cat value can be calculated through the following equation [48]:
(2)
i
H 2
O 2
i
P B S
=
π
1 / 2
K
c a t
∙ c ∙ t
1 / 2

At a given concentration of H2O2, a plot of i(H2O2)/i(PBS) versus t1/2 must be a linear one. Accordingly, K cat of 55.5 M−1s−1 could be estimated from the slope of the plot at 2 mM (Fig. 4C). Finally, the current response versus time was monitored with the successive addition of H2O2 showed in Fig. 4D. The current response linearly increased with H2O2 concentration, revealing the quantitative reduction of H2O2 catalyzed by N-doped C-PDANTs.

Based on the discussion above, we can speculate that similar electron transfer pathway on the modified electrode might involve in catalytic reaction in TMB-N-doped C-PDANTs-H2O2 system. On one hand, it is well known that the pyridinic N can create the active sites on its adjacent carbon atoms for ORR, making them Lewis bases for reducing the energy barriers of the oxygen absorption and promoter for the electron transfer. On the other hand, the graphitic N doped in graphene planes was demonstrated to induce a nonuniform electron distribution and form short CN bond, which can enhance oxygen absorption and weaken OO bonds [14,49]. Thereby such capabilities conferred by either pyridinic N or graphitic N are expected in TMB-N-doped C-PDANTs-H2O2 system. Because of the relative high affinity of N-doped C-PDANTs to TMB, the electron transfer from amine groups of TMB to N-doped C-PDANTs occurs and increases the electron density and electron donating property of N-doped C-PDANTs. Consequently, the N dopants accelerate the electron transfer from N-doped C-PDANTs to the absorbed H2O2, leading to the reductive decomposition of H2O2 and the generation of OH● radicals [47,50].

Due to the efficient quenching of TMB oxidation by GSH, N-doped C-PDANTs with peroxidase-like activity were applied to the GSH detection. GSH is a non-nucleic acid and thiolated tripeptide widespread in eukaryotic cells of a living being, participating in various physiological activities, such as anti-oxidation, immunity, DNA regulation, etc [51,52]. Also, abnormal GSH level is closely related to many clinical diseases, including heart disease, Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer’s disease, and cancer [53–55]. Thus, a simple and sensitive method for GSH is highly expected to fulfill the fast development in clinical diagnosis [56]. In this respect, we have developed a colorimetric method for GSH based on TMB-N-doped C-PDANTs-H2O2 system. UV–vis spectra were recorded by adding GSH at different concentrations to TMB-N-doped C-PDANTs-H2O2 system as shown in Fig. S9. The absorbance at 652 nm decreased gradually with increasing GSH concentration. This indicates that oxTMB can be reduced by GSH to form colorless TMB and GSSG (Fig. 5 A and S10). Then by plotting the absorbance change (ΔA) as a function of GSH concentration, we could observe that the ΔA increased with increasing GSH concentration in Fig. 5B. The linear calibration curve was fitted in the concentration range of GSH from 0.6–60 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.991 (Fig. 5C). The detection limit for GSH was calculated to be 0.2 μM with a signal to noise ratio of 3. The comparisons of different peroxidase-like nanomaterials and methods for GSH detection were showed in Tables S4 and S5, respectively. The data reveal that the proposed method based on N-doped C-PDANTs is a competitive one for the sensitive detection of GSH.

The selectivity of this proposed method for GSH detection was tested by possible interferential substances. The absorbance quenching efficiency for each analyte was measured using the same system and the results were compared in Fig. S11. The data indicate that the quenching efficiency of GSH is much higher than that of the interferential substance. Exceptionally, the presence of the other reducing agent, like L-Cys or ascorbic acid (AA), at high concentration might induce a strong absorbance quenching. But the concentration of L-Cys or AA (micromolar level) in biological samples is far lower than the concentration of GSH (millimolar level). And the reducing ability of L-Cys or AA is weaker than that of GSH [57–59]. Therefore the interference of L-Cys or AA can be negligible. To verify the applicability of this proposed method for GSH detection, human serum samples were analysis after dilution with 0.2 M acetate buffer solution (pH4.0). The average concentration of GSH among three 150-fold diluted serum samples was measured to be 3.7 μM by our proposed method. Then by spiking a given amount of GSH in diluted serum sample, the total concentration of GSH was measured by the same approach. The data listed in Table 1 indicate that the good recoveries and RSD can be obtained in the detections.

In summary, we have prepared the highly N-doped C-PDANTs after the carbonization of PDA formed on the sacrificial HNTs templates. These porous N-doped C-PDANTs show high peroxidase-like activity and can catalyze the chromogenic reaction of TMB and H2O2, while negligible oxidase-like, catalase-like and SOD-like activities were found in the relevant substrate systems. Such intrinsic catalytic mechanism was deciphered by electrochemical measurements. N-doped C-PDANTs/Nafion GCE enabled H2O2 reduction to occur at less negative potential due to the efficient electron transfer accelerated by pyridinic N and graphitic N in N-doped C-PDANTs, providing further understanding of the peroxidase-like activity of N-doped carbon materials. Moreover, these N-doped C-PDANTs were successfully exploited to detect GSH through the colorimetric method with high sensitivity. Finally, the superiorities of N-doped C-PDANTs lie in easy preparation, low-cost, high stability, and biocompatibility, making them competent for enzyme-mimic application. Considering the strong metal-ion chelating of PDA, co-doping with other metal atoms should guide the design of new type of enzyme-like nanomaterials with higher catalytic activity or tandem catalysis.

Tian Zhang conducted the experiments. Haidong Li conceived the idea and wrote the paper. Miaoxia Liu, Han Zhou, Zhicheng Zhang, Yu Cao analyzed the data. Chengyin Wang and Guoxiu Wang revised, reviewed and edited the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundations of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20180893).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129987.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Charged carbon nanomaterials: redox chemistries of fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and graphenes
Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.J. Clancy, M.K. Bayazit, S.A. Hodge, N.T. Skipper, C.A. Howard, M.S.P. Shaffer, Charged carbon nanomaterials: Redox chemistries of fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and graphenes, Chem. Rev. 118 (2018) 7363-7408.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tian Zhang is a master student of Yangzhou University. Her main research areas are electrochemical sensor based on carbon nanomaterials.

Haidong Li received his PhD degree in Physical Chemistry from University of Bordeaux in 2017 and is currently working at the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on the electrochemical sensor, electrochemiluminscence (ECL) analysis and functional polymer materials.
Miaoxia Liu is a master student of Yangzhou University. Her main research areas are fluorescent sensors and ECL sensors based on nanomaterials.
Han Zhou is a master student of Yangzhou University. Her main research areas are electrochemical sensor based on DNA-modified electrodes.
Zhicheng Zhang is a master student of Yangzhou University. His research interests focus on the preparation of stimuli-responsive hydrogel.

Yu Cao received his PhD degree from Jilin University in 2016 and is currently working at the College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on the preparation of metal − organic frameworks (MOFs) and their applications in electrochemical sensor.

Chengyin Wang received his PhD degree in physical chemistry from Yangzhou University in 2007. He is currently a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Yangzhou University. His research interests focus on analytical chemistry for materials, functional materials, and electrochemical sensors.

Guoxiu Wang received his PhD degree in Materials Science and Engineering in 2001 from University of Wollongong, Australia. He currently is working as a Distinguished Professor at Faculty of Sciences, University of Technology Sydney, Australia, and a director of Centre for Clean Energy Technology. His major research interests include nanostructured functional materials, materials chemistry in energy storage and conversion, and development of chemical and biological sensors.
